summary: Compared the user's implementable OS improvement plan document against the current DinoBrain app/data state. Verified the document SHA-256, inspected P0/P1 work packages, read current data status artifacts, and ran the full closed-loop verifier. Found that full-memory audit, review/semantic settlement, task lifecycle/finish gate, RAG proof/eval, OS memory growth, and data safety rails are partly or currently healthy, while real Codex live pre-response proof, status freshness self-reference/index refresh, native/runtime authority gates, route/evidence/owner mutation gate, claim lineage closure, Korean graph-first UI proof, release/new-PC recovery, multi-PC sync safety, wiki lint/proposal settlement, and outcome metrics remain below the document's final bar.
decisions: Treat the comparison as evidence-based against the current local app/data state, not only the plan text.
Do not claim full final OS readiness because verify:goal still fails on real Codex Desktop live pre-response proof and status freshness still reports stale artifacts.
Treat local_text_hashing_v1 dense vectors as a deterministic RAG proof artifact, not as production semantic embeddings.
next_steps: Restart/repair stale DinoBrain MCP processes and produce a fresh Codex Desktop live proof event, then rerun npm run verify:goal.
Fix status freshness self-reference/index refresh so full-memory audit, SQLite manifest, and graph health can remain fresh after monitoring writes.
Implement the remaining document gates: authority scans, mutation gate, source lineage closure, UI proof, release/new-PC recovery, and multi-PC sync safety.
